1: Paul's arrival in Corinth after Athens marks the transition from philosophical proclamation (the Areopagus) to the urban church-planting that will produce his longest community and most extensive correspondence. The Christological continuity: the same risen Christ who is 'the wisdom of God' (1 Corinthians 1:24) — Paul's counter to Corinthian philosophizing — is proclaimed in the city that prizes wisdom above all. The Christological mission does not avoid intellectual centers but enters them precisely because Christ is the answer to the deepest questions they raise.
2: Aquila and Priscilla's expulsion from Rome under Claudius's edict connects the Christological community to the Jewish-Roman political sphere. They were expelled because of Christ-related synagogue disputes — the Christological presence in Rome's Jewish community preceded Paul's arrival there by years. Every missionary 'coincidence' in Acts is the risen Christ pre-positioning his witnesses. Aquila and Priscilla are the instruments through whom the Corinthian and Ephesian communities are seeded before Paul even arrives.
3: Paul's tent-making alongside Aquila and Priscilla establishes the Christological model of self-supporting ministry. The apostle's refusal to accept payment from the Corinthians (1 Corinthians 9:12-18) is a deliberate Christological statement: unlike the sophists and wonder-workers who sold their wisdom, the gospel is offered without charge because Christ gave himself without charge. The cross is the pattern: 'he who was rich became poor for your sake' (2 Corinthians 8:9). Working with his hands is gospel-shaped economics.
4: Paul's synagogue reasoning every Sabbath — with both Jews and Greeks — is the Christological method: beginning where the Scriptures are read, arguing from those Scriptures that Jesus is the Messiah. The double audience (Jews with Torah preparation, Greeks with philosophical hunger) is Corinth's demographic reality. The risen Christ is simultaneously the fulfillment of Jewish messianic expectation and the true wisdom that Greek philosophical seeking dimly pursued (1 Corinthians 1:22-24).
5: 'Paul was occupied with the word, testifying to the Jews that the Christ was Jesus' (synecheto tō logō diamartyromenos tois Ioudaiois einai ton Christon Iēsoun) — the Christological testimony formula: the Christ (the expected Messiah of Israel's scripture) is Jesus (the specific historical individual who died and rose). The identification is the simplest and most explosive claim in the NT — it connects the whole weight of Israel's canonical hope to a specific person. Paul was 'pressed by the word' (synecheto, constrained, compelled) — the Christological urgency is Spirit-driven, not merely rhetorical.
6: The shaking of garments and declaration 'your blood be on your own heads; I am innocent' transfers the Christological responsibility of response. The Christological truth that Paul proclaimed had been offered and rejected; the consequence of that rejection belongs to the rejecters. The image is Ezekielian (Ezekiel 33:4-9): the watchman who warns is absolved when the warning is given. Christ himself wept over Jerusalem's rejection (Luke 19:41-44); the apostle's gesture is the extension of that Christological sorrow into apostolic practice.
7: The move to Titius Justus's house — next door to the synagogue — is the Christological community's formation outside the established institution. The house-church adjacent to the synagogue is the NT's characteristic spatial relationship: the new community grows alongside and from the old, not as its enemy but as its fulfillment. The Christological community takes root where the Scriptures are read and the messianic hope lives, even when the institution rejects the Messiah's arrival.
8: Crispus the synagogue ruler believed with his whole household — the conversion of the institution's leader is the Christological reversal the parable of the tenants describes (Luke 20:9-19): the stone rejected becomes the cornerstone. The ruler who administered the very Sabbath proclamations that Paul argues from now believes their Christological fulfillment. Paul himself baptized Crispus (1 Corinthians 1:14) — the personal apostolic connection signals the significance of this conversion for the Corinthian community's foundation.
9: A direct revelation: 'Do not be afraid, but go on speaking and do not be silent, for I am with you' — the risen Christ's night vision to Paul in Corinth is a direct Christological assurance. 'I am with you' (egō eimi meta sou) echoes the Great Commission promise (Matthew 28:20 — 'I am with you always, to the end of the age') and the patriarchal reassurances (Genesis 26:24; Acts 28:15; Isaiah 41:10). The Christ who commands also accompanies; the commission is never given without the promise of presence. The vision sustains the eighteen-month Corinthian ministry.
10: A direct revelation: 'For I have many in this city who are my people' (laos esti moi polys en tē polei tautē) — Christ's declaration of his hidden people in Corinth is the Christological election claim. 'My people' (laos) is the LXX covenant-people term; Christ is declaring that Corinth has people whom he has already claimed as his own before they have heard the gospel. The Christological sovereignty over election precedes the apostolic proclamation; the mission reveals what the election has already determined. This is the Christological ground of missionary confidence in hard places.
11: Eighteen months teaching the word of God — the longest recorded stay in any city except Ephesus — is the Christological investment whose fruit is 1 Corinthians and 2 Corinthians. The extended residency produces the most theologically complex Pauline correspondence. The Christological depth of the Corinthian letters (1 Corinthians 1:18-2:16; Acts 15; 2 Corinthians 5:14-21) is inseparable from the depth of the community-formation that eighteen months of teaching produced. Time in the community is the Christological patient work that produces mature witness.
12: The Gallio hearing — the Roman proconsul's refusal to adjudicate Jewish theological disputes — is the Christological mission's first formal Roman legal vindication. Paul has done 'nothing wrong' according to Roman law; the claims about Jesus are not criminal matters but internal Jewish religious questions. The Christological significance: the risen Christ's lordship is not threatening to the Roman order (the accusation of the persecutors is false); the empire's proper legal function inadvertently protects the Christological mission.
13: The accusation — 'this man is persuading people to worship God contrary to the law' — is simultaneously false (Paul teaches the fulfillment of the Torah) and revealing (the gospel does relativize religious institutions). The Christological community is always accused of subversion by those whose identity is invested in the institutions the gospel fulfills and transcends. The 'contrary to the law' charge is the same one brought against Jesus (Luke 23:2) and Stephen (Acts 6:13) — the pattern of false accusation follows the pattern of the Master.
14: Gallio's decisive intervention — before Paul can speak — establishes the Roman legal pattern: the gospel is legally innocent by the empire's own standards. The Christological significance of Gallio's ruling runs through the rest of Acts: the Roman frame consistently acquits the gospel and its messengers. Whether this is providential protection or apologetic argument (Luke demonstrating the legality of the faith to Roman readers), the result is the same: Christ's witnesses are not lawbreakers but those whom unjust accusers have tried unsuccessfully to frame.
15: Gallio's ruling — 'a matter of questions about words and names and your own law' — is the Roman administrator's precise description of the Christological dispute: it concerns the name and identity of Jesus and how he relates to Israel's law. The Christological content is accurately identified even if its significance is missed: the dispute is exactly about 'the name' — the one name by which salvation comes (Acts 4:12). Gallio dismisses what he cannot understand; his dismissal is nevertheless Christologically accurate about the nature of the question.
16: Gallio's physical expulsion of the accusers from the tribunal — he 'drove them from the judgment seat' — is the Roman legal system's active repudiation of the persecution. The Christological pattern reverses: those who sought to condemn the gospel-bearer are themselves driven away by the very authority they invoked. The risen Christ's sovereignty over earthly courts means that attempts to use institutional power against his mission consistently produce unexpected reversals.
17: Sosthenes — beaten by the crowd, possibly the successor to Crispus — may be the same Sosthenes who appears in 1 Corinthians 1:1 as Paul's co-author. If so, the man beaten for failing to convict Paul becomes Paul's co-worker. The Christological transformation pattern: the adversary becomes the companion; the persecutor becomes the witness. The same pattern that converted Paul himself recurs in lesser instances throughout Acts. No one is so fixed in opposition to Christ that the risen Lord cannot turn them.
18: Paul's vow at Cenchreae — cutting his hair as part of a Nazirite-like commitment — demonstrates his continued observance of Jewish religious practice. The Christological significance: Paul, the apostle of Gentile freedom from Torah, himself voluntarily observes Jewish practices. This is the Christological freedom of 1 Corinthians 9:19-22 — 'I have become all things to all people.' The risen Christ's lordship includes freedom both to observe and not to observe cultural practices; what matters is serving the Christological mission, not the practices themselves.
19: The brief synagogue visit in Ephesus — Paul leaves Priscilla and Aquila, enters the synagogue and reasons with the Jews — is the planting of the seed that will bear fruit in the three-year Ephesian ministry of Acts 19. The Christological mission plants and waters according to its own timing; not every contact produces immediate harvest. The Ephesians' positive response ('they asked him to stay longer') shows receptivity that will be developed when Paul returns.
20: The Ephesians' desire that Paul remain longer is the Christological invitation that Paul declines — for now. The Christological mission has its own calendar; not every open door is entered immediately. Paul's restraint here, with the promise to return (Acts 18:21), mirrors the Christological pattern of the delayed parousia: the risen Lord's timing cannot be forced by human desire, even well-intentioned desire. The promise to return, kept in Acts 19, is itself a Christological lesson in patient trust.
21: Paul's departure after promising 'I will return to you if God wills' — the Christological qualification of all planning. 'If God wills' (tou theou thelontos) is the language of Christological surrender to divine sovereignty over the mission's direction. James 4:15 will make this into an explicit principle: 'you ought to say, If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that.' The apostle's plans are always provisional; the risen Christ's governance of the mission is always determinative.
22: The brief Jerusalem greeting and descent to Antioch closes Paul's second missionary journey. The Christological significance of returning to Jerusalem: the apostle maintains connection with the mother-community whose foundation in the resurrection appearances grounds the entire Christological proclamation. The periodic Jerusalem returns are not bureaucratic check-ins but Christological renewals — the living community of resurrection witnesses is the ongoing source of apostolic authority.
23: Paul's third journey begins with strengthening the disciples through Galatia and Phrygia — the pastoral revisitation of planted communities. The Christological pattern of apostolic ministry: not only proclamation (extending the mission) but consolidation (deepening the Christological understanding of those already converted). The risen Christ's command is to 'make disciples' (Matthew 28:19), not merely decisions; the strengthening visits are the discipleship component of the Great Commission.
24: Apollos's arrival in Ephesus — an eloquent Alexandrian, instructed in the way of the Lord, fervent in spirit — brings the Christological proclamation with intellectual depth from a different tradition. The Christological community has breadth: it is not limited to one apostolic lineage but is gathered by the Spirit from every tradition that encounters the risen Christ. Apollos represents the Alexandrian Jewish intellectual stream that the Christological mission absorbs and transforms.
25: Apollos was 'instructed in the way of the Lord' and 'speaking and teaching accurately the things concerning Jesus, though he knew only the baptism of John' — the Christological accuracy of his teaching coexists with an incompleteness about the Spirit's full arrival. The Christological community includes those whose understanding is partial but genuine; the appropriate response is not rejection but completion. Priscilla and Aquila's supplementing of Apollos's knowledge is the model of how the Christological community handles partial but sincere faith.
26: Priscilla and Aquila 'took him aside and explained to him the way of God more accurately' — the Christological completion of Apollos's proclamation is achieved through private instruction by a lay couple, not by apostolic authority. The Christological community's teaching function is not restricted to apostles; Spirit-filled believers who have received the full Christological understanding can complete the formation of gifted preachers. The Holy Spirit's work through Priscilla and Aquila is as Christologically authoritative as any apostolic instruction.
27: The Ephesian church's letter commending Apollos to Corinth — the first recorded apostolic letter of commendation — extends the Christological network across geographic and cultural boundaries. The risen Christ's community is inter-connected; trust is transferable through the bonds of Christological fellowship. Apollos's Corinthian ministry, 'greatly helping those who had believed through grace' and 'powerfully refuting' Jewish objections, shows how the Christological mission grows through multiple gifted witnesses working in sequence.
28: Apollos 'powerfully refuted the Jews in public, demonstrating by the Scriptures that the Christ was Jesus' — the Christological method is Scripture-demonstration: the existing canon, rightly read, points to the specific person who fulfilled it. The same argument Paul makes in Romans 1-4 and Galatians 3-4, Apollos makes publicly in Corinth. The risen Christ is the key that unlocks the OT — every major Christological argument in the NT is fundamentally a demonstration that the OT was always pointing to Jesus.
